title: Overfitting and Underfitting
concept:
**Overfitting** and **underfitting** are the two principal ways an ML model can fail to generalise correctly.

**Overfitting**: The model learns the training data too well — including noise and random fluctuations — resulting in excellent performance on training data but poor performance on new data.

*Detection*: Significantly worse performance on the **test dataset** compared to the **validation dataset**. The accuracy gap between validation and test is the primary indicator.

*Learning curve signature*: Training accuracy is high; validation accuracy is lower and diverges further from training accuracy as training continues.

*Causes*: Model too complex (too many parameters for the available data), insufficient training data, insufficient regularisation.

*Mitigations*: Reduce model complexity, add Dropout/L2 regularisation, obtain more training data, use early stopping.

**Underfitting**: The model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data. Performance is poor on both training AND validation data.

*Detection*: Low accuracy on BOTH training and validation datasets. Learning curves for both remain high and close together, neither improving significantly.

*Causes*: Model too simple (too few parameters), insufficient training time, over-regularisation.

*Mitigations*: Increase model complexity, train for more epochs, reduce regularisation, improve feature engineering.

**Right-fitting**: The model generalises well — training and validation performance are both high and close to each other.

These conditions must be distinguished because their mitigations are opposite: overfitting needs simplification; underfitting needs complexification.
keyPoints: Overfitting produces high training accuracy but significantly worse test accuracy — the model memorised training noise and fails to generalise.
Underfitting produces low accuracy on BOTH training and validation datasets — the model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns.
The mitigations are opposite: overfitting → reduce complexity/add regularisation; underfitting → increase complexity/train longer.

quiz.question: A model achieves 99% accuracy on the training dataset and 62% accuracy on the test dataset. What condition does this describe, and what is the most appropriate mitigation?
options: Underfitting — the model needs more layers and training epochs.
Right-fitting — the 37% gap is acceptable because test data is always harder than training data.
Overfitting — the model has memorised training data; mitigations include reducing model complexity, adding regularisation, or obtaining more training data.
Data drift — the test dataset has a different distribution from the training dataset.
quiz.explanation: A 99% training accuracy with 62% test accuracy is a classic overfitting signature — the enormous gap indicates the model memorised training data rather than learning generalizable patterns. Underfitting would show low accuracy on BOTH datasets. Data drift might explain a smaller gap at deployment time but not during initial model testing with a properly held-out test set.
